expanded_description:
Expectations play a crucial role in the Phillips curve and in macroeconomics more broadly. How people form expectations about future inflation determines the dynamics of the Phillips curve, the cost of disinflation, and the effectiveness of monetary policy. The two main competing theories are adaptive expectations (expectations are based on past inflation) and rational expectations (expectations use all available information, including future policy announcements). The difference between these theories has profound implications for whether monetary policy can systematically surprise people, whether disinflations must cause recessions, and whether the Phillips curve trade-off can be exploited by policymakers.

Understanding the role of expectations is essential for analyzing the credibility of central banks, the costs of disinflation, and the Lucas critique (that econometric models estimated under one policy regime may not hold under another). Adaptive expectations dominated macroeconomics in the 1960s and 1970s; they explained the accelerationist hypothesis and the Volcker disinflation (which required a recession). Rational expectations, developed by Robert Lucas (1972) and Thomas Sargent (1976), challenged the idea that policymakers could systematically exploit the Phillips curve. If expectations are rational, only unexpected policy changes affect output; anticipated policy has no real effects. This implies that credible disinflation can be costless (no recession).

In this section, we will examine adaptive expectations (formation, implications for the Phillips curve, accelerationist hypothesis), rational expectations (formation, Lucas critique, policy ineffectiveness proposition), the role of credibility in disinflation, empirical evidence on expectations formation, and the implications for monetary policy (inflation targeting, forward guidance). You will learn to distinguish between adaptive and rational expectations, understand why the sacrifice ratio depends on expectations, and evaluate the conditions under which disinflation can be costless.

core_concept.principle:
Expectations about future inflation are a key determinant of the Phillips curve. The two main theories are adaptive expectations and rational expectations:

Adaptive Expectations:
• Expectations are formed based on past inflation.
• Simplest form: $\pi^e_t = \pi_{t-1}$ (expect last period's inflation to continue).
• More general: $\pi^e_t = \theta \pi_{t-1} + (1-\theta) \pi^e_{t-1}$ (weighted average).
• Implication: Expectations adjust slowly; there are persistent forecast errors.
• Phillips curve with adaptive expectations: $\pi_t = \pi_{t-1} - \beta(u_t - u_n)$.
• This implies the accelerationist hypothesis: to keep $u < u_n$, inflation must accelerate.
• Disinflation requires a recession (unemployment above $u_n$).

Rational Expectations (Lucas, Sargent):
• Expectations are formed using all available information, including knowledge of the economic model and future policy.
• Forecast errors are random (unpredictable) on average.
• $\pi^e_t = E[\pi_t | \Omega_{t-1}]$, where $\Omega_{t-1}$ is all information available at time $t-1$.
• Implication: Systematic monetary policy cannot surprise people; only unexpected policy affects output.
• Policy ineffectiveness proposition (Sargent-Wallace): Anticipated monetary policy has no effect on real output.
• Credible disinflation can be costless (no recession) if the central bank's announcement is believed.
• Lucas critique: Econometric models estimated under one policy regime may not hold when policy changes, because expectations change.

Comparison:
| Feature | Adaptive Expectations | Rational Expectations |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Information used | Past inflation only | All available information |
| Forecast errors | Persistent (systematic) | Random (unpredictable) |
| Disinflation cost | Positive sacrifice ratio | Zero if credible |
| Can policy systematically surprise? | Yes | No |
| Lucas critique | Does not apply | Policy changes change expectations |

Credibility:
• The cost of disinflation depends on credibility.
• If the central bank is credible, $\pi^e$ falls quickly, and the sacrifice ratio is low.
• If the central bank lacks credibility, $\pi^e$ adjusts slowly, and the sacrifice ratio is high.
• The Volcker disinflation (1979-1982) had a high sacrifice ratio because the Fed lacked credibility.
• The 1990s disinflations had lower sacrifice ratios because central banks had built credibility.

Policy Implications:
• Central banks should build credibility to reduce the cost of disinflation.
• Inflation targeting is a framework for anchoring expectations.
• Forward guidance works by influencing expectations directly.
• The Lucas critique implies that policy rules should be robust to changes in expectations.
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine two economies with the same Phillips curve but different expectations formations.

In the adaptive expectations economy, people expect inflation to be the same as last year. The central bank wants to reduce inflation from 5% to 2%. It raises interest rates, causing unemployment to rise to 8%. Inflation falls to 3% this year. But people still expect 5% inflation (because they look at last year's 5%). Next year, the central bank must keep unemployment high to bring inflation down further. The process takes years and costs a recession. This is the Volcker disinflation.

In the rational expectations economy, the central bank announces that it will reduce inflation from 5% to 2%. Because the central bank is credible, people immediately lower their inflation expectations to 2%. The Phillips curve shifts down. The central bank can reduce inflation without raising unemployment. Disinflation is costless. This is the 'cold turkey' disinflation that Sargent argued could have happened in the 1980s if the Fed had been credible.

In reality, the Volcker disinflation was costly because the Fed lacked credibility. By the 1990s, the Fed had built credibility, and the 1994-1995 disinflation (soft landing) was much less costly. The role of expectations is central to modern monetary policy: central banks use inflation targeting and forward guidance to anchor expectations and reduce the cost of stabilizing inflation.

image.alt: Two graphs: Adaptive expectations (disinflation requires recession) vs. Rational expectations (credible disinflation can be costless).
image.caption: Figure 10.3.1: The role of expectations in disinflation. Under adaptive expectations, reducing inflation requires a recession. Under rational expectations, credible disinflation can be costless.

sections.introduction:
Expectations play a crucial role in the Phillips curve and in macroeconomics more broadly. How people form expectations about future inflation determines the dynamics of the Phillips curve, the cost of disinflation, and the effectiveness of monetary policy. The two main competing theories are adaptive expectations (expectations are based on past inflation) and rational expectations (expectations use all available information, including future policy announcements). The difference between these theories has profound implications for whether monetary policy can systematically surprise people, whether disinflations must cause recessions, and whether the Phillips curve trade-off can be exploited by policymakers.

Understanding the role of expectations is essential for analyzing the credibility of central banks, the costs of disinflation, and the Lucas critique (that econometric models estimated under one policy regime may not hold under another). Adaptive expectations dominated macroeconomics in the 1960s and 1970s; they explained the accelerationist hypothesis and the Volcker disinflation (which required a recession). Rational expectations, developed by Robert Lucas (1972) and Thomas Sargent (1976), challenged the idea that policymakers could systematically exploit the Phillips curve. If expectations are rational, only unexpected policy changes affect output; anticipated policy has no real effects. This implies that credible disinflation can be costless (no recession).

In this section, we will examine adaptive expectations (formation, implications for the Phillips curve, accelerationist hypothesis), rational expectations (formation, Lucas critique, policy ineffectiveness proposition), the role of credibility in disinflation, empirical evidence on expectations formation, and the implications for monetary policy (inflation targeting, forward guidance).

introduction_image.alt: Timeline: 1960s (adaptive expectations dominant), 1970s (rational expectations developed), 1980s (Volcker disinflation, credibility debate), 1990s-2020s (inflation targeting, forward guidance).
introduction_image.caption: Figure 10.3.2: Historical evolution of expectations theory in macroeconomics: from adaptive to rational expectations.

key_concept.definition:
Expectations: Beliefs about future economic variables (inflation, output, interest rates) that influence current behavior (wage setting, price setting, consumption, investment).

Adaptive Expectations:
• Expectations are formed based on past inflation.
• Simple form: $\pi^e_t = \pi_{t-1}$
• General form: $\pi^e_t = \theta \pi_{t-1} + (1-\theta) \pi^e_{t-1}$, where $0 < \theta < 1$
• Implication: Expectations adjust slowly; there are systematic forecast errors.
• Phillips curve: $\pi_t = \pi^e_t - \beta(u_t - u_n)$
• With $\pi^e_t = \pi_{t-1}$: $\pi_t - \pi_{t-1} = -\beta(u_t - u_n)$ (accelerationist Phillips curve).

Rational Expectations (Lucas 1972, Sargent 1976):
• Expectations are formed using all available information, including knowledge of the economic model and future policy.
• $\pi^e_t = E[\pi_t | \Omega_{t-1}]$, where $\Omega_{t-1}$ is all information at time $t-1$.
• Forecast errors are random (unpredictable) on average: $E[\pi_t - \pi^e_t | \Omega_{t-1}] = 0$.
• Implication: Systematic monetary policy cannot surprise people; only unexpected policy affects output.
• Policy ineffectiveness proposition (Sargent-Wallace): Anticipated monetary policy has no effect on real output.
• Credible disinflation can be costless: if the central bank announces a lower inflation target and is believed, $\pi^e$ falls immediately, and the SRPC shifts down, allowing disinflation without recession.

Lucas Critique (1976):
• Econometric models estimated under one policy regime may not hold when policy changes, because expectations change.
• Example: A Phillips curve estimated during a period of stable inflation may not predict inflation when the central bank changes policy.
• Implication: Policymakers cannot use historical correlations to evaluate policy changes; they need models with microfoundations (expectations based on policy).

Credibility:
• The degree to which economic agents believe the central bank will achieve its stated goals.
• High credibility → expectations adjust quickly → low sacrifice ratio.
• Low credibility → expectations adjust slowly → high sacrifice ratio.
• Credibility is built over time through consistent policy actions.

Empirical Evidence:
• The Volcker disinflation (1979-1982) had a high sacrifice ratio (3-4), consistent with adaptive expectations (slow adjustment).
• The 1990s disinflations (e.g., New Zealand, Canada) had lower sacrifice ratios, consistent with rational expectations (credibility).
• Survey data (Michigan Survey, SPF) show that expectations are not purely adaptive; they respond to news and policy announcements.
• Most economists believe that expectations are 'rational' in the sense of using all available information, but with some stickiness (sticky information).
key_concept.context: The role of expectations in macroeconomics was transformed by the rational expectations revolution of the 1970s. Before Lucas, most models used adaptive expectations (or ignored expectations entirely). Lucas argued that rational expectations were necessary for microfoundations: if people are rational, they should not systematically make forecast errors. The policy ineffectiveness proposition challenged Keynesian activism. The Lucas critique led to the development of DSGE models with rational expectations. Today, most central bank models use rational expectations, but with modifications (sticky information, learning). The debate between adaptive and rational expectations has largely been resolved in favor of rational expectations, but the speed of adjustment (credibility) remains a key policy concern.

image.alt: Diagram: Adaptive expectations (past inflation → expected inflation) vs. Rational expectations (all information → expected inflation).
image.caption: Figure 10.3.3: Adaptive expectations use only past inflation; rational expectations use all available information, including future policy.

classifications.value: Expectations are formed based on past inflation; expectations adjust slowly, leading to persistent forecast errors
characteristics: $\pi^e_t = \pi_{t-1}$ (simplest form) or weighted average of past inflation.
Disinflation requires a recession (positive sacrifice ratio).
The Volcker disinflation (1979-1982) is consistent with adaptive expectations.
classifications.behavior:
Under adaptive expectations, people form expectations based on what inflation was in the past. If inflation has been 5% for several years, they expect 5% next year. If the central bank suddenly reduces inflation to 2%, people will be surprised. They will continue to expect 5% for some time, only gradually adjusting their expectations downward as they observe lower inflation. This slow adjustment means that disinflation requires a prolonged period of high unemployment to 'teach' people that inflation is lower.

The accelerationist Phillips curve ($\pi_t - \pi_{t-1} = -\beta(u_t - u_n)$) implies that to keep unemployment below the natural rate, inflation must accelerate. To keep unemployment at 4% when $u_n = 5\%$, inflation must rise by $\beta$ each period. This is the accelerationist hypothesis.

Adaptive expectations were the standard assumption in macroeconomics from the 1960s through the early 1970s. They explained the 1970s stagflation: the Fed's attempts to keep unemployment low led to accelerating inflation. They also predicted that disinflation would require a recession, which was borne out by the Volcker experience.

However, adaptive expectations have been criticized for assuming that people ignore information about future policy. If the central bank announces a credible disinflation plan, rational agents would lower their expectations immediately, not wait to see lower inflation. This is the rational expectations critique.

image.alt: Graph: Actual inflation (line) and adaptive expectations (dashed line); expectations lag behind actual inflation, creating systematic forecast errors.
image.caption: Figure 10.3.4: Under adaptive expectations, expectations lag behind actual inflation. Forecast errors are systematic (persistent).

examples.explanation: Inflation was 9% in 1979. The Fed raised rates, causing unemployment to rise to 10.8% in 1982. Inflation fell to 3% by 1983. But inflation expectations (survey-based) adjusted slowly; even in 1982, expected inflation was still 5-6%. The slow adjustment of expectations is consistent with adaptive expectations. The sacrifice ratio was high (3-4).

If $u_n = 5\%$, $\beta = 0.5$, and the Fed wants to keep unemployment at 4%, then $\pi_t - \pi_{t-1} = -0.5(4 - 5) = 0.5$. Inflation must rise by 0.5 percentage points each year. After 10 years, inflation would be 5% higher. This is unsustainable; eventually the Fed must allow unemployment to rise.

classifications.value: Expectations are formed using all available information; forecast errors are random; anticipated policy has no real effects
characteristics: Forecast errors are random (unpredictable) on average.
Policy ineffectiveness proposition: Anticipated monetary policy has no effect on real output.
Credible disinflation can be costless (zero sacrifice ratio).
Lucas critique: Models must account for changes in expectations when policy changes.
classifications.behavior:
Under rational expectations, people use all available information to form expectations. They know the central bank's policy rule, the structure of the economy, and current economic conditions. They do not make systematic forecast errors; errors are random and unpredictable.

The policy ineffectiveness proposition (Sargent-Wallace) states that anticipated monetary policy has no effect on real output. If the central bank announces a money supply increase, people anticipate the resulting inflation and adjust wages and prices immediately. Real variables (output, employment) do not change. Only unexpected policy shocks affect output.

This has radical implications for disinflation: if the central bank announces a credible disinflation plan, people immediately lower their inflation expectations. The Phillips curve shifts down, and inflation falls without any increase in unemployment. The sacrifice ratio can be zero.

The Lucas critique argues that econometric models estimated under one policy regime (e.g., a period of stable inflation) are not valid for evaluating a different policy regime (e.g., a disinflation). Because expectations change with policy, the parameters of the model (like the Phillips curve slope) may change. Policymakers need models with microfoundations and rational expectations to evaluate policy changes.

While the strong version of rational expectations (perfect information, no frictions) is controversial, most modern macroeconomic models (DSGE) incorporate rational expectations with some frictions (sticky prices, sticky information).

image.alt: Graph: Actual inflation and rational expectations; expectations are unbiased; forecast errors are random around zero.
image.caption: Figure 10.3.5: Under rational expectations, expectations are unbiased; forecast errors are random and unpredictable.

examples.explanation: The central bank announces that inflation will be 2% next year. Because it is credible, people immediately set inflation expectations to 2%. The Phillips curve shifts down. The central bank can reduce inflation from 5% to 2% without raising unemployment. The sacrifice ratio is zero. This is the rational expectations prediction.

New Zealand adopted inflation targeting in 1990 with a highly credible framework. When the central bank tightened, inflation expectations fell quickly, and the disinflation was less costly than in countries without credibility. This is consistent with rational expectations, though not perfectly costless.

classifications.value: The cost of disinflation depends on the credibility of the central bank; credible disinflations have lower sacrifice ratios
characteristics: Credibility: The degree to which agents believe the central bank will achieve its targets.
High credibility → expectations adjust quickly → low sacrifice ratio.
Low credibility → expectations adjust slowly → high sacrifice ratio.
Credibility is built over time through consistent policy actions.
Inflation targeting is a framework for building credibility.
Forward guidance works by directly influencing expectations.
classifications.behavior:
The sacrifice ratio is not a fixed parameter; it depends on the credibility of the central bank. A credible central bank can reduce inflation at lower cost because expectations adjust quickly. A central bank that lacks credibility must create a deep recession to convince people that inflation is really coming down.

The Volcker disinflation (1979-1982) had a high sacrifice ratio (3-4) because the Fed lacked credibility. The public had experienced the Great Inflation of the 1970s and did not believe that Volcker would stick to tight policy. It took a deep recession to convince them.

By the 1990s, the Fed had built credibility. The 1994-1995 disinflation (soft landing) had a much lower sacrifice ratio (1-2). The public believed that the Fed would prevent inflation, so expectations adjusted quickly.

Inflation targeting (adopted by many central banks in the 1990s) is a framework for building credibility. By announcing a numerical inflation target (e.g., 2%) and being held accountable, central banks anchor expectations. This reduces the cost of disinflation and makes inflation more stable.

Forward guidance is another tool for influencing expectations directly. The central bank announces its future policy path (e.g., 'rates will stay low until inflation averages 2%'). If credible, this shapes expectations and affects long-term interest rates even without changing current policy.

image.alt: Graph: Sacrifice ratio over time (1970s high, 1990s low); Volcker disinflation (high credibility cost); 1990s disinflations (low cost).
image.caption: Figure 10.3.6: The sacrifice ratio depends on central bank credibility. The Volcker disinflation had a high sacrifice ratio because the Fed lacked credibility. By the 1990s, credibility was higher, and disinflations were less costly.

examples.explanation: The Fed had low credibility in 1979. Inflation expectations were adaptive and slow to adjust. The sacrifice ratio was estimated at 3-4. Reducing inflation by 6 percentage points (from 9% to 3%) cost about 18-24% of GDP in lost output.

By 1994, the Fed had built credibility through two decades of fighting inflation. When the Fed raised rates preemptively, inflation expectations remained anchored. The sacrifice ratio was low (1-2). The Fed raised rates without causing a recession—a 'soft landing.'

principles.principle: The Lucas Critique: Econometric Models Estimated Under One Policy Regime Are Not Valid for Evaluating Policy Changes
principles.statement: Robert Lucas (1976) argued that traditional econometric models (estimated using historical data) are not reliable for evaluating policy changes because the parameters of the model (like the Phillips curve slope) are not structural; they depend on expectations, which change when policy changes. For example, a Phillips curve estimated during a period of stable inflation may have a certain slope. But if the central bank changes policy (e.g., announces a disinflation), expectations will change, and the slope may change. Using the old estimated relationship to predict the effects of the new policy would be misleading. The Lucas critique led to the development of DSGE models with microfoundations and rational expectations. These models are designed to be 'Lucas-critique-proof' because they derive behavioral equations from optimizing agents who form expectations rationally. The implication for policymakers is that they cannot rely on historical correlations to predict policy effects; they need structural models that account for changes in expectations.
principles.application: Central banks should use structural models (DSGE) with rational expectations, not reduced-form models estimated from historical data, to evaluate policy changes. Forecasts based on historical Phillips curves may be unreliable if the policy regime changes. The Fed's FRB/US model and the BoC's ToTEM model are examples of structural models that incorporate rational expectations. Policymakers should also be cautious about using the sacrifice ratio estimated from past disinflations to predict the cost of a future disinflation; credibility matters.
principles.example: In the 1960s, the Phillips curve appeared stable. Policymakers believed they could choose a point on the curve (e.g., 3% unemployment with 2% inflation). When the Fed tried to exploit the trade-off, expectations changed, and the Phillips curve shifted. The historical relationship broke down. This is the Lucas critique in action: the estimated Phillips curve was not structural; it depended on the policy regime.

image.alt: Graph: Phillips curve estimated under regime A (stable inflation) and shifted under regime B (changing inflation expectations); Lucas critique: parameters change with policy.
image.caption: Figure 10.3.7: The Lucas critique: parameters estimated under one policy regime (e.g., stable inflation) may not hold when policy changes because expectations change.

principles.principle: Credible Central Banks Can Reduce the Cost of Disinflation by Anchoring Expectations
principles.statement: The cost of disinflation (the sacrifice ratio) is not fixed; it depends on the credibility of the central bank. A credible central bank can reduce inflation at lower cost because expectations adjust quickly. In the extreme (rational expectations), credible disinflation can be costless. In practice, credibility reduces but does not eliminate the sacrifice ratio. Central banks build credibility through consistent policy actions, transparency, and accountability. Inflation targeting is a framework designed to anchor expectations and build credibility. Forward guidance is a tool for directly influencing expectations. The lesson from the Volcker disinflation is that low credibility leads to high costs; the lesson from the 1990s is that credibility matters. Modern central banks invest heavily in communication (press conferences, minutes, forecasts) to shape expectations and maintain credibility.
principles.application: Central banks should prioritize building and maintaining credibility. This means: (1) setting clear inflation targets, (2) being transparent about policy decisions, (3) being accountable (e.g., requiring the governor to explain deviations from target), and (4) following through on commitments. Forward guidance should be credible; if the central bank says rates will stay low, it must actually keep them low (unless conditions change). Inflation targeting has been successful in anchoring expectations in many countries (Canada, UK, Sweden, Australia, New Zealand). The Fed adopted average inflation targeting in 2020 to enhance credibility at the zero lower bound.
principles.example: In 1991, New Zealand adopted inflation targeting with a highly credible framework (the Reserve Bank Act made the governor accountable for inflation outcomes). When the central bank tightened, inflation expectations fell quickly, and the disinflation was less costly than in countries without credibility. Canada adopted inflation targeting in 1991 and also saw reduced sacrifice ratios.

image.alt: Graph: Inflation expectations (survey measures) anchored around 2% target; deviations are temporary; credibility keeps expectations stable.
image.caption: Figure 10.3.8: Credible central banks anchor inflation expectations around the target. This reduces the cost of disinflation and makes inflation more stable.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Disinflation Costs Under Adaptive vs. Rational Expectations

image.alt: Table: Adaptive expectations (slow adjustment, high unemployment) vs. Rational expectations (immediate adjustment, no unemployment).
image.caption: Figure 10.3.9: Worked example: comparing the path of unemployment and inflation under adaptive vs. rational expectations during a disinflation.

worked_examples.problem:
An economy has the Phillips curve $\pi_t = \pi^e_t - 0.5(u_t - u_n)$ with $u_n = 5\%$. Initial inflation is 8%, and expected inflation is 8% (adaptive) or 8% (but will adjust under rational). The central bank wants to reduce inflation to 2% over 2 years.

Case A (Adaptive Expectations): $\pi^e_t = \pi_{t-1}$.
Case B (Rational Expectations): $\pi^e_t = E[\pi_t | \Omega_{t-1}]$, and the central bank is credible.

a) For Case A (adaptive), calculate the unemployment rate needed in year 1 to reduce inflation from 8% to 5% (a 3 percentage point reduction).
b) For Case A, calculate the unemployment rate needed in year 2 to reduce inflation from 5% to 2%.
c) For Case B (rational, credible), explain what happens to inflation and unemployment when the central bank announces the disinflation.
d) Calculate the cumulative unemployment loss (in percentage points above natural rate) for each case.
e) Why do the costs differ?

step_by_step.step: Case A, Year 1: Reduce inflation from 8% to 5%
step_by_step.explanation:
Adaptive expectations: $\pi^e_1 = \pi_0 = 8\%$. Target $\pi_1 = 5\%$. Phillips curve: $5 = 8 - 0.5(u_1 - 5)$.

$0.5(u_1 - 5) = 8 - 5 = 3$
$u_1 - 5 = 3 / 0.5 = 6$
$u_1 = 5 + 6 = 11\%$

Unemployment must rise to 11% in year 1 (6 percentage points above natural rate).

step_by_step.step: Case A, Year 2: Reduce inflation from 5% to 2%
step_by_step.explanation:
After year 1, actual inflation was 5%, so $\pi^e_2 = \pi_1 = 5\%$. Target $\pi_2 = 2\%$.

$2 = 5 - 0.5(u_2 - 5)$
$0.5(u_2 - 5) = 5 - 2 = 3$
$u_2 - 5 = 6$
$u_2 = 11\%$

Unemployment must rise to 11% again. The sacrifice ratio is high because expectations adjust slowly.

The central bank announces that inflation will be 2% in both years. Because it is credible, people immediately set $\pi^e = 2\%$ for both years. The Phillips curve is $\pi_t = 2 - 0.5(u_t - 5)$.

If the central bank sets $\pi_t = 2\%$, then $2 = 2 - 0.5(u_t - 5)$ → $0 = -0.5(u_t - 5)$ → $u_t = 5\%$.

Inflation falls to 2% immediately without any increase in unemployment. The disinflation is costless.

Case A: Year 1: unemployment 6 points above natural rate; Year 2: another 6 points above. Cumulative = 12 percentage-point-years of excess unemployment.

Case B: Unemployment always at natural rate. Cumulative excess unemployment = 0.

The cost difference is dramatic: adaptive expectations require a deep recession; rational expectations (with credibility) require no recession.

The costs differ because of how expectations adjust. Under adaptive expectations, expectations are backward-looking; people only update their expectations after observing actual inflation. So to reduce inflation, the central bank must create a recession to 'teach' people that inflation is lower. Under rational expectations with credibility, people anticipate the disinflation; they lower their expectations immediately. The Phillips curve shifts down, and inflation falls without any recession. The key is credibility: if people believe the central bank will achieve the lower inflation target, the disinflation is costless. If they do not believe (low credibility), the central bank must prove its commitment through a recession.

In reality, the Volcker disinflation (1979-1982) had a high cost because the Fed lacked credibility. The public did not believe that Volcker would stick to tight policy. It took a deep recession to convince them. By the 1990s, the Fed had built credibility, and the 1994-1995 disinflation (soft landing) was much less costly.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) u1 = 11%. b) u2 = 11%. c) Inflation falls to 2% immediately; u = 5% (no recession). d) Case A: 12 percentage-point-years; Case B: 0. e) Adaptive expectations require a recession to adjust expectations; rational expectations (credibility) allow immediate adjustment.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying adaptive vs. rational expectations to calculate the unemployment cost of disinflation and understanding the role of credibility.

worked_examples.title: Lucas Critique: Policy Change and Parameter Instability

image.alt: Table: Pre-1970 Phillips curve (stable), Post-1970 Phillips curve (shifted); Lucas critique explains why historical estimates fail.
image.caption: Figure 10.3.10: Worked example: the Lucas critique applied to the Phillips curve breakdown of the 1970s.

worked_examples.problem:
An economist estimates the Phillips curve using data from 1950-1969 and finds a stable relationship: $\pi = 2.5 - 0.3(u - 5)$. The natural rate is estimated at $u_n = 5\%$.

a) Based on this estimated Phillips curve, what inflation rate would be associated with unemployment of 4%?
b) What inflation rate would be associated with unemployment of 6%?
c) In the 1970s, the actual data showed that when unemployment was 4%, inflation was 8% (not the predicted 2.8%). Why did the estimated Phillips curve fail?
d) How does the Lucas critique explain this failure?
e) If the Fed had used the estimated Phillips curve to set policy in the 1970s, what would have happened?

step_by_step.step: Calculate inflation at u=4% using estimated curve
Explain why the Phillips curve failed in the 1970s
step_by_step.explanation: In the 1970s, when unemployment was 4%, actual inflation was 8%, far above the predicted 2.8%. The Phillips curve had shifted up. Why? Because expected inflation had risen. In the 1950s-1960s, expected inflation was stable around 2%. In the 1970s, after years of expansionary policy, expected inflation rose to 6-8%. The Phillips curve relationship is $\pi = \pi^e - \beta(u - u_n)$. The estimated curve from the 1950s-1960s had $\pi^e \approx 2.5\%$. In the 1970s, $\pi^e$ was much higher, so the curve shifted up. The historical relationship was not structural; it depended on the policy regime (which affected expectations).

The Lucas critique states that econometric models estimated under one policy regime are not valid for evaluating policy changes because the parameters of the model are not structural; they depend on expectations, which change when policy changes. In the 1950s-1960s, the Fed followed a policy of low inflation (implicitly). Expectations were anchored. The estimated Phillips curve captured the relationship under that regime. In the 1970s, the Fed changed policy (expansionary, trying to lower unemployment). This changed expectations, shifting the Phillips curve. The old estimated relationship no longer held. Policymakers who relied on the old curve would have been misled.

The Lucas critique implies that policymakers need structural models (with microfoundations and rational expectations) that are 'Lucas-critique-proof'—models where the parameters do not change with policy. The Phillips curve slope $\beta$ may be structural (related to the degree of price stickiness), but the intercept $\pi^e$ is not; it depends on policy. A structural model would incorporate expectations formation explicitly, allowing the intercept to change when policy changes.

step_by_step.step: What would have happened if the Fed used the old Phillips curve?
step_by_step.explanation: If the Fed had used the estimated Phillips curve from the 1950s-1960s to set policy in the 1970s, it would have believed that it could lower unemployment to 4% with only a small increase in inflation (from 2.5% to 2.8%). The Fed would have pursued expansionary policy, lowering unemployment. But because expectations had risen, the actual Phillips curve was much higher. As the Fed expanded, inflation would have risen sharply (to 8% or higher), not just to 2.8%. The Fed would have been surprised by high inflation and would have had to tighten aggressively, causing a recession. This is essentially what happened: the Fed under Arthur Burns pursued expansionary policy, inflation rose, and the Fed had to tighten under Volcker. The Lucas critique explains why the historical estimates were misleading.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) 2.8%. b) 2.2%. c) Expected inflation rose, shifting the Phillips curve up. d) Lucas critique: parameters depend on expectations, which change with policy regime. e) The Fed would have been surprised by high inflation and would have had to tighten, causing a recession.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the Lucas critique to understand why historical Phillips curve estimates failed in the 1970s and why structural models are needed for policy evaluation.

process_flow.description: Measure actual inflation (CPI, PCE) and expected inflation (surveys, TIPS breakevens, market-based measures).
process_flow.mechanism: Actual π from BLS; expected π from Michigan Survey, SPF, or TIPS.

image.alt: Diagram: Inflation data, survey expectations, market expectations → observed π and π^e.
image.caption: Figure 10.3.11: Step 1 - Observe actual inflation and expected inflation from surveys and market-based measures.

process_flow.process: Test for forecast errors (are errors systematic?)
process_flow.description: Check whether forecast errors (π - π^e) are random (unpredictable) or systematic (autocorrelated).
process_flow.mechanism: If errors are random → rational expectations; if systematic (e.g., π^e consistently lags π) → adaptive expectations.

image.alt: Graph: Forecast errors over time; if mean zero and uncorrelated → rational; if persistent → adaptive.
image.caption: Figure 10.3.12: Step 2 - Test whether forecast errors are systematic (adaptive expectations) or random (rational expectations).

process_flow.description: Determine whether the central bank is credible (expectations anchored around target) or not (expectations unanchored).
process_flow.mechanism: If π^e close to target and stable → credible; if π^e volatile and deviates from target → low credibility.

image.alt: Graph: Inflation expectations anchored around 2% target (credible) vs. moving with actual inflation (low credibility).
image.caption: Figure 10.3.13: Step 3 - Assess central bank credibility by examining whether inflation expectations are anchored around the target.

process_flow.description: Based on expectations formation and credibility, predict the cost of reducing inflation.
process_flow.mechanism: If expectations adaptive or credibility low → high sacrifice ratio; if rational and credible → low or zero sacrifice ratio.

image.alt: Decision tree: Adaptive expectations or low credibility → high sacrifice ratio; Rational expectations and high credibility → low sacrifice ratio.
image.caption: Figure 10.3.14: Step 4 - Predict the cost of disinflation based on expectations formation and central bank credibility.

process_flow.process: Design policy to anchor expectations (inflation targeting, forward guidance)
process_flow.description: Use policy tools to influence expectations directly and build credibility.
process_flow.mechanism: Inflation targeting: announce numerical target, be transparent, be accountable. Forward guidance: communicate future policy path.

image.alt: Diagram: Inflation targeting, forward guidance, transparency → anchored expectations → lower disinflation cost.
image.caption: Figure 10.3.15: Step 5 - Design policy to anchor expectations and build credibility, reducing the cost of disinflation.

image.alt: Master framework: Observe expectations → test forecast errors → assess credibility → predict disinflation cost → design policy to anchor expectations.
image.caption: Figure 10.3.16: Complete framework for analyzing the role of expectations in the Phillips curve and monetary policy.

summary: Expectations play a crucial role in the Phillips curve and monetary policy. Adaptive expectations assume that people form expectations based on past inflation ($\pi^e_t = \pi_{t-1}$). This leads to the accelerationist Phillips curve ($\pi_t - \pi_{t-1} = -\beta(u_t - u_n)$) and implies that disinflation requires a recession (positive sacrifice ratio). Rational expectations (Lucas, Sargent) assume that people use all available information, including knowledge of future policy. Forecast errors are random, not systematic. The policy ineffectiveness proposition states that anticipated monetary policy has no effect on real output. Credible disinflation can be costless (zero sacrifice ratio) because expectations adjust immediately. The Lucas critique argues that econometric models estimated under one policy regime are not valid for evaluating policy changes because expectations change with policy. Empirical evidence suggests that expectations are not purely adaptive; they respond to policy announcements. Credibility matters: the Volcker disinflation (low credibility) had a high sacrifice ratio; 1990s disinflations (higher credibility) had lower sacrifice ratios. Modern central banks use inflation targeting and forward guidance to anchor expectations and build credibility. Understanding the role of expectations is essential for analyzing the costs of disinflation, evaluating policy changes, and designing effective monetary policy frameworks.
